description: §6.6.10 — two V5-strict derived cells whose initializers reference each other (`const <a> = @b + 1` / `const <b> = @a + 1`) form a mutual circular derived dependency. This fires E-DERIVED-CIRCULAR-DEP as a fatal error → the CLI exits non-zero (exit 1), failing the build at the deploy gate. (Codegen still RUNS and writes the cyclic `_scrml_derived_subscribe` pair to `dist/*.client.js` per the uniform CLI write-on-error contract — the protection is the non-zero exit that blocks deployment of that infinite-recompute artifact, NOT artifact-withholding.) Regression guard for Rx-1: the detector previously only saw the legacy `@`-form (structuralForm:false) derived cells; the V5-strict `const <x> = expr` form (structuralForm:true) was routed through the plain-state collector, so its dep edges were never built and the cycle compiled clean in the real pipeline.
rationale: §6.6.10: a derived cell whose RHS depends (directly or transitively) on a cell whose RHS depends back on it is a circular derived dependency — an infinite recompute loop. Both the legacy `@`-form and the V5-strict `const <x> = expr` form are derived cells (both emit `_scrml_derived_subscribe`), so both must participate in the derived-reads cycle subgraph. E-DERIVED-CIRCULAR-DEP is a fatal error (§34) → the compiler exits non-zero (exit 1), failing the build at the deploy gate. (The cyclic JS is still emitted to `dist/*.client.js` per the uniform CLI write-on-error contract; the non-zero exit — not artifact-withholding — is what keeps the infinite-recompute artifact from being deployed.)
